## About

It's always slightly awkward to write your own bio. ;) I grew up in Oregon with entrepreneurial parents. I started my career in gaming and fell into product management shortly after college. That led me to San Francisco where I worked in gaming for the first few years of my career before starting Product Hunt. Fast-forward to today, I spend most of my time investing in early-stage startups out of Weekend Fund and live in Miami (although I spend a fair amount of time working remotely in LA, NYC, and other places with fast WiFi). Outside of work I enjoy live music, hanging with friends in smaller groups (introvert 🙋🏼‍♂️), and weightlifting.

Previously I served as the CEO and Chief Change Catalyst for The B Team, where global business and civil society leaders work together on confronting our crisis of conformity in leadership. I have long been on a quest to inspire and empower leaders to embrace purpose-driven, gender-balanced and principled leadership. I have passionately pursued this purpose as a business leader in Corporate America with M&M and Pepsi Cola, as the first female CEO of Iceland’s Chamber of commerce and as an executive and non-executive director for diverse businesses. I have an international MBA from Thunderbird and have lived and worked in the US, the UK and across the Nordics. My work has led me to the TED stage several times and I have delivered keynotes and participated in dialogues about new visions for leadership for companies and conferences around the world. Newsweek named me to a list of 150 women who “shake the world” and the New Yorker called me a “Living Emoji of Sincerity”. Who needs a formal title when you can be an Emoji?

It was also the prelude to an incredible story, one of perseverance, accomplishment, and enduring spirit in the face of once unthinkable challenges. His message for young people, academia and the corporate community alike emphasizes character, resiliency, service, kindness and turning challenges into strengths.

After playing professional soccer in the U.S. and Zimbabwe, Zohn went on to win the CBS reality show, Survivor Africa in 2001. He used his prize money to Co-found the organization Grassroot Soccer (GRS) that uses the power of soccer to educate, inspire and mobilize communities to improve health. Ethan has been a force in driving the strategic direction of GRS and building partnerships that have helped to provide lifesaving education for more than 25 million youth in 60+ different countries. As global spokesperson, Ethan launched GRS UNITED, a U.S. awareness campaign, by dribbling a soccer ball 550 miles from Boston to Washington, D.C.

Ethan became an unlikely face of cancer. Diagnosed not once but twice with CD20+ Hodgkin’s Lymphoma in the span of several years, Ethan endured years of aggressive treatment, including two stem cell transplants. Never losing optimism, spirit, or humor despite extraordinary rigors and setbacks, Ethan used his journey and considerable platform to connect with others.

As demonstrated by his charitable work with various global and domestic health and wellness organizations, and community involvement, Ethan believes that a better and healthier world can be achieved by living with strong values. Ethan travels the world as an inspirational speaker emphasizing importance of kindness, community and a connection to social goodness.

Ethan ran the NY & Boston Marathon and was named a global ambassador for Cycle For Survival, Leukemia & Lymphoma Society and StandUp2Cancer. He is a medical cannabis advocate and ambassador for EO Care, which provides clinician-created cannabis products for cancer. Zohn has been featured in and hosted the Emmy nominated show, Everyday Health on ABC, Outside Today on the Outside Television Network and returned to reality TV as a contestant on Survivor: Winners at War (2019), The Amazing Race, Survivor All-Star, Fear Factor, Eco-Challenge and co-wrote, “Soccer World,” a series of books for young readers to explore the world in a compelling way through soccer. A Guide for Decision-Makers.” Taking place on #WorldCitiesDay, the event opened by Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General, and led by Dr Etienne Krug and Dr Nathalie Roebbel, brought together leaders across sectors to shape a shared vision for healthier, more resilient cities. In his opening remarks, Dr Ghebreyesus emphasized the importance of a strategic approach to urban health amidst a range of health threats and rapid urbanization. Speaking on the panel “Taking the urban health agenda forward,” alongside urban health leaders, Kobie Brand, ICLEI Deputy Secretary General, emphasized that urban health can be mainstreamed and scaled through three key actions: 1️⃣ Strategic frameworks that connect the dots. The WHO Guide helps cities act systemically, linking health outcomes with planning, housing, energy, and mobility — and offers a shared language for sectors that too often work apart. 2️⃣ Financing that values health co-benefits. Climate and development finance mechanisms must recognize the enormous health returns on investment. Integrating health into climate finance can unlock new resources and resilience dividends. 3️⃣ Capacity and partnerships that empower cities. The overarching goal of the SOR4D is to produce knowledge and innovative solutions by needs-driven, transdisciplinary research. The project aims to enhance the “social resilience” of subsistence farming communities, especially women living in rural areas of the Ganges-Brahmaputra delta, on the outskirts of the Sundarbans Forest. Women in these communities face multiple challenges resulting from climate-induced hazards. Despite these adversities, their roles, particularly in providing emotional support to their families during times of crisis, remain both significant and irreplaceable. Recognizing these contributions, the project actively engages marginalized women in collective, large-scale farming initiatives that emphasize sustainable agricultural practices. Participants utilized homemade fertilizers and pesticides during cultivation, resulting in substantial yields of beetroots alongside various other vegetables. These outcomes reflect a meaningful shift in women's resilience capacities within coastal Bangladesh. Moreover, this project examines how small-scale farmers in Pratapnagar, Satkhira, Bangladesh, perceive their social vulnerability and their exposure to multi-hazards such as floods, storm surges and salinization, and builds a comparative synthesis of their challenges based on archival research, household surveys, and ethnographic fieldwork. To support rural communities in this socio-ecologically fragile region, the project implements “real-life labs” as experimental spaces to translate knowledge into actionable community-driven solutions. These labs explore sustainable agroecological practice followed by marketing by upscaling the products, guided by local ecological knowledge and community collaboration. Initial findings reveal that agroecological practices require collective action and institutional support for long-term sustainability, and innovative strategies for resource management, while market access emerges as a significant obstacle for rural communities. As demonstrated by his charitable work with various global and domestic health and wellness organizations, and community involvement, Ethan believes that a better and healthier world can be achieved by living with strong values. Ethan travels the world as an inspirational speaker emphasizing importance of kindness, community and a connection to social goodness.

The project aims to enhance the “social resilience” of subsistence farming communities, especially women living in rural areas of the Ganges-Brahmaputra delta, on the outskirts of the Sundarbans Forest.
